question: What is a 'business role' in S/4HANA and how does it conceptually differ from a classic ECC single role?
hints: Think about what content gets attached to the role menu beyond transactions
Consider embedded vs two-tier deployment
expectedAnswer: A business role is still a PFCG role technically, but its menu is built from business catalogs and groups (bundles of Fiori apps/tiles) rather than only transaction codes, and it is designed to represent a job function across both classic GUI and Fiori consumption in one container.
detailedAnswer: Structurally a business role is generated exactly like any other PFCG role - it produces a profile and generated authorizations the same way. The difference is conceptual and content-driven: instead of a menu of transaction codes, SAP delivers pre-built business catalogs (containers of Fiori app tiles and their target mappings) and business groups that you assign into the role menu alongside or instead of classic transactions. In embedded deployment (the S/4HANA default), one business role menu can mix classic SAP GUI transactions and Fiori catalogs, and PFCG's SU24-driven default proposal mechanism still runs underneath to populate the authorization objects for both.
followUps: Does assigning a business catalog to a role menu generate different content than adding a transaction code?
What changes about business role design in a hub deployment versus embedded?
commonMistakes: Assuming business roles are a fundamentally different technical object from PFCG roles
Forgetting that classic transaction-code-based authorization design still applies underneath Fiori content
scenario: A functional lead asks why the project still uses PFCG for Fiori business roles when the client was told S/4HANA security would be 'completely different.'

question: Explain the hierarchy between a business catalog, a business group, and a business role, and what each one actually stores.
hints: One is a read-only container of tiles/target mappings
One is purely an organizational grouping for the launchpad menu
expectedAnswer: A business catalog is a container of Fiori app tiles and their target mappings (largely SAP-delivered and treated as read-only reference content); a business group is an arrangement of tiles for launchpad presentation; a business role is the PFCG role that references catalogs (and optionally groups) in its menu and is what actually gets assigned to users.
detailedAnswer: Business catalogs (e.g., SAP-delivered IDs following a naming pattern per module) bundle related app tiles with their semantic object/action target mappings - they are content, not authorization, and are best treated as read-only reference objects that you reference rather than modify directly, copying to a Z-namespace only when true customization is needed. Business groups control how catalogs' tiles are organized for launchpad display and are largely superseded by spaces/pages in newer Fiori 3 launchpads but still exist for backward compatibility. The business role in PFCG is the only one of the three that is actually assigned to users; when you drag a catalog into a business role's menu, PFCG treats each tile's target mapping like a transaction start authorization and runs the SU24 default-proposal mechanism to pull in the backend OData/business authorization objects the app needs.
followUps: Why would you copy a standard SAP catalog into a custom Z-catalog instead of using it directly?
How does assigning a group differ from assigning a catalog directly to a role menu?
commonMistakes: Modifying SAP-delivered catalogs directly instead of copying to a custom namespace
Believing groups are still the primary launchpad organizing concept in a spaces/pages-based launchpad
scenario: A security team wants to add two custom tiles to a standard SAP-delivered business catalog and is unsure whether that's the correct approach.

question: Explain how CDS view-based DCL (Data Control Language) row-level security works and how its enforcement point differs from a classic AUTHORITY-CHECK.
hints: Think about a WHERE clause bound to an authorization object
Consider where in the stack the filtering actually happens - ABAP layer or database layer
expectedAnswer: DCL attaches a role definition to a CDS view using a GRANT SELECT ... WHERE clause whose condition is populated by an 'aspect pfcg_auth' reference to a classic authorization object and field, so that row-level filtering is pushed down and applied automatically to every consumer of the view - unlike a classic AUTHORITY-CHECK, which is an explicit statement a developer must code and which returns pass/fail rather than filtering rows.
detailedAnswer: A CDS view marked with the annotation that activates authorization checking gets a companion DCL source: `DEFINE ROLE <name> USING <cds_view> { GRANT SELECT ON <cds_view> WHERE (field) = aspect pfcg_auth(AUTHOBJECT, FIELD, ACTVT = '03'); }`. At runtime, whenever any consumer reads the view - a Fiori OData service, an ABAP report, an embedded analytics query, CDS-to-CDS association - the generated WHERE condition is injected transparently, filtering rows the user isn't authorized to see, rather than throwing a hard authorization dump. This is fundamentally different from a classic AUTHORITY-CHECK, which is an explicit ABAP statement placed at one code entry point that returns sy-subrc pass/fail and stops execution; it doesn't filter a result set, it gates access to a whole operation. DCL row-level security is reusable across every consumer of the view automatically, which is powerful but also means a badly designed DCL condition silently under- or over-filters data everywhere the view is used, with no single AUTHORITY-CHECK statement you can grep for to find the logic.
followUps: What happens if a CDS view has no DCL role assigned at all - is it open or blocked by default?
How would you unit test that a DCL condition filters correctly across every consumption path (OData, analytics, CDS association)?
commonMistakes: Assuming DCL throws an authorization error like AUTHORITY-CHECK instead of silently filtering rows
Not realizing one flawed DCL condition affects every consumer of the CDS view, not just one app
scenario: A custom analytical Fiori app built on a CDS view is returning zero rows for some users and all rows for others, with no error message on screen.
diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant User
  participant OData as OData/Fiori Service
  participant CDS as CDS View + DCL
  participant Buffer as User Auth Buffer
  User->>OData: Request entity set
  OData->>CDS: SELECT on CDS view
  CDS->>Buffer: Evaluate aspect pfcg_auth(AUTHOBJECT, FIELD)
  Buffer-->>CDS: Authorized field values
  CDS-->>OData: Rows filtered by WHERE condition
  OData-->>User: Only authorized rows returned (no error)

question: How do analysis authorizations for embedded analytics differ from classic authorization objects, and when are they actually required?
hints: Think about aggregation-based queries versus row-level OData reads
Consider 0BI_ALL and RSECADMIN
expectedAnswer: Analysis authorizations are a BW/embedded-analytics-specific authorization model built from InfoObject characteristic value combinations and maintained in RSECADMIN rather than PFCG; they're required whenever a query aggregates data through the embedded analytics engine (CDS-based queries, Analytical List Pages, KPI tiles) rather than doing a plain transactional row read, because classic AUTHORITY-CHECK objects don't participate in the OLAP engine's authorization evaluation.
detailedAnswer: Classic authorization objects (M_, F_, V_ series) are evaluated by AUTHORITY-CHECK statements embedded in transactional ABAP code paths. Embedded analytics - Analytical List Pages, Overview Page KPI cards, and any CDS view consumed as an analytical query - runs through the OLAP/analytics engine, which evaluates a completely separate authorization model: analysis authorizations, built from InfoObject characteristic values (e.g., 0COMP_CODE, 0PROFIT_CTR) and maintained via RSECADMIN rather than PFCG, referencing S_RS_AUTH under the hood. Every analysis authorization automatically carries technical characteristics like 0TCAACTVT and 0TCAIPROV; the special value 0BI_ALL grants unrestricted access across all characteristics and should be treated with the same caution as SAP_ALL - it's a common finding in security assessments when granted broadly for convenience during go-live and never revoked. A user can have full classic authorization to view a table's data transactionally and still see nothing on an analytical tile because analysis authorizations were never assigned for the relevant characteristics.
followUps: How would you design analysis authorizations to align with an existing company-code-based classic authorization scheme?
What's the audit risk of granting 0BI_ALL broadly during a go-live crunch?
commonMistakes: Assuming classic PFCG role authorizations automatically cover embedded analytics access
Granting 0BI_ALL as a shortcut and never scoping it down post-go-live
scenario: A finance user with full FI transactional access reports that an Overview Page KPI tile for their company code shows no data at all.

question: A single CDS-based data model is consumed both by a transactional Fiori app (via OData) and by an embedded analytics KPI tile. What governance challenge does this create for row-level security, and how would you address it?
hints: Two different authorization models are now both in play for the same underlying data
Think about keeping DCL and analysis authorizations aligned as org structures change
expectedAnswer: The same CDS view is now subject to two independently maintained authorization mechanisms - DCL/classic authorization objects for the transactional consumption path and analysis authorizations for the analytical path - and if they aren't deliberately kept aligned (same org values, same maintenance triggers), a user can end up with inconsistent visibility depending on which app they open.
detailedAnswer: DCL row-level security is enforced through classic authorization objects and evaluated per the pfcg_auth aspect regardless of consumption path in principle, but embedded analytics queries route through the OLAP engine which layers analysis authorizations on top, evaluated independently against InfoObject characteristic values. In practice this means the transactional app can show a user data filtered correctly by company code via DCL, while the analytical tile on the very same data shows either nothing (analysis authorization never provisioned) or too much (0BI_ALL granted as a shortcut) because nobody wired the two provisioning processes together. The fix is architectural: treat org-level provisioning as a single source of truth - when a user's company code/profit center scope changes, the same event should trigger both the classic role's org value update and the corresponding RSECADMIN analysis authorization update, ideally through a shared identity governance workflow rather than two disconnected manual processes maintained by different teams.
followUps: Who typically owns analysis authorization maintenance versus classic role maintenance in a real organization, and why does that split cause drift?
How would you build a reconciliation check to detect divergence between the two models?
commonMistakes: Treating DCL and analysis authorizations as automatically synchronized because they sit on the same CDS view
Letting two different teams maintain org-level scope for the same data without a shared process
scenario: After a reorganization, a regional controller can correctly see only their new company code's transactional postings but still sees the old company code's data on their analytics dashboard.

question: When migrating an ECC role landscape to S/4HANA, what's the practical difference between a brownfield and a greenfield approach to role redesign, and how do you decide between them?
hints: Think about how much of the existing role structure survives
Consider timeline and risk tolerance as deciding factors
expectedAnswer: Brownfield keeps the existing role structure largely intact, remapping retired transaction codes to their Fiori app equivalents and layering in catalogs/groups on top of the legacy design; greenfield redesigns roles from scratch around S/4HANA business processes and Fiori app catalogs, discarding legacy structure entirely - the choice depends on how healthy the existing role landscape already is and the project's risk/timeline tolerance.
detailedAnswer: Brownfield migration is lower-risk and faster: you keep the existing PFCG role structure, use the SAP Fiori apps reference library and transaction-to-app mapping reports to identify which classic transactions have Fiori equivalents, and layer business catalogs into existing role menus alongside legacy transactions - viable when the current role landscape is reasonably clean and not itself a known liability. Greenfield redesign starts from S/4HANA business processes and standard SAP business role templates, discarding legacy role structure entirely - the right call when the existing landscape carries years of SoD violations, naming inconsistency, or excessive role sprawl that would simply be carried forward (and calcified) by a brownfield lift-and-shift. The practical decision driver is almost always: is the current role landscape an asset worth preserving, or a liability the migration project is the only realistic opportunity to fix, given how disruptive a full redesign is once the business is live on S/4HANA.
followUps: What signals in an ECC role landscape assessment would push you firmly toward greenfield despite the extra time and cost?
How would you handle a hybrid approach where only certain modules get greenfield treatment?
commonMistakes: Defaulting to brownfield purely to save time without assessing whether the legacy landscape is actually healthy enough to carry forward
Choosing greenfield without a realistic timeline/budget for the scale of redesign involved
scenario: A client with a decade-old, heavily customized ECC role landscape is planning an S/4HANA conversion and asks whether roles can simply be 'carried over.'

question: During an ECC-to-S/4HANA migration, how would you find and assess authorization object changes introduced by SAP's Simplification List, and why can't you rely on SU25 alone?
hints: SU25 reconciles SU24 defaults, not structural object changes shipped by simplification
Think about custom code that hard-codes AUTHORITY-CHECK against an object SAP changed
expectedAnswer: SU25 only reconciles SU24 default proposals between old and new SAP-shipped values for existing check IDs; it does not tell you where SAP's Simplification List obsoleted, replaced, or changed the field structure of an authorization object entirely, so you must separately review the Simplification List and run custom code impact analysis to find AUTHORITY-CHECK statements against objects that no longer behave the same way.
detailedAnswer: SU25's comparison logic operates within the existing SU24 default-value framework - useful for catching newly proposed or changed check indicators for transactions you already use, but it has no visibility into a scenario where SAP's Simplification List retires a transaction/data model entirely (e.g., classic MM-IM movement types superseded by new logic) or changes an authorization object's field structure as part of that simplification. The correct approach layers three checks: review the relevant Simplification Item catalog entries for the modules in scope to identify authorization-relevant changes explicitly called out by SAP, run a custom code impact analysis (ABAP Test Cockpit / custom code migration checks) specifically looking for AUTHORITY-CHECK statements against objects flagged as changed or obsoleted, and only then use SU25 to reconcile the SU24 defaults for whatever remains. Skipping the Simplification List review and relying on SU25 alone means custom code can silently pass or fail authorization checks against an object whose semantics changed under the hood, discovered only in production.
followUps: How would you prioritize which Simplification Items to review first for a large, multi-module conversion?
What's the risk of a custom AUTHORITY-CHECK statement against an object SAP marked obsolete but didn't remove from the system?
commonMistakes: Treating SU25 as a complete migration authorization impact assessment on its own
Not cross-referencing custom code AUTHORITY-CHECK statements against the Simplification List at all
scenario: Three weeks after an S/4HANA conversion go-live, a custom Z-report that previously worked in ECC starts throwing an unexpected authorization dump for an object nobody had changed in years.

question: How would you sequence and govern the security workstream in a greenfield S/4HANA implementation so that business catalog/group scoping doesn't become a late-project bottleneck?
hints: Think about when Fiori app decisions get made relative to functional design
Consider engaging business process owners early rather than after functional design freezes
expectedAnswer: Start business role and catalog scoping in parallel with functional process design rather than after it, run structured workshops with business process owners to confirm which Fiori apps map to each process step early, and set a governance checkpoint that freezes catalog/group scope before the build phase so security design doesn't become dependent on a functional design that keeps shifting late in the project.
detailedAnswer: The most common cause of security becoming the critical-path bottleneck late in an S/4HANA program is that catalog and business role scoping only starts after functional design is 'done,' by which point there's no runway left to properly workshop role scope with business process owners. The better sequencing runs business role/catalog scoping as a parallel track alongside functional process design, using the Fiori apps reference library to map process steps to apps as functional decisions are made, not after. Structurally, put a governance checkpoint - a formal scope freeze - between functional design and build, where business process owners sign off on the business catalog/group-to-role mapping; changes after that point go through a change-control process rather than ad hoc rework, which is what actually protects the build and test timeline from continuous re-scoping.
followUps: How would you handle a business process owner who wants to keep changing role scope well into the build phase?
What metrics would tell you the security workstream is at risk of becoming the critical path?
commonMistakes: Starting security design only after functional design is fully frozen
Not establishing a formal scope-freeze checkpoint, leading to continuous late-stage rework
scenario: A steering committee is asking why the security team keeps requesting scope changes two weeks before the start of integration testing.

question: Design an enterprise governance model for role and Fiori content lifecycle across a hybrid landscape of multiple S/4HANA instances plus SAP BTP extension apps.
hints: Think about what stays centrally standardized versus what each instance/app needs to own locally
Consider how transport works differently for PFCG roles versus Fiori launchpad content versus BTP role collections
expectedAnswer: Define a central framework covering naming conventions, catalog/group content standards, and a common identity/SoD model that spans all S/4 instances and BTP apps, while delegating instance-specific role authoring locally within that framework, and explicitly architect the transport chain for each content type - PFCG roles, Fiori launchpad content, and BTP role collections/scopes - since they don't move through the landscape the same way or on the same cadence.
detailedAnswer: A hybrid landscape of several S/4HANA instances plus BTP extension apps has at least three distinct content types that must be governed together but don't share a single lifecycle: classic PFCG roles/profiles moved via standard ABAP transports, Fiori launchpad content (catalogs, groups, spaces/pages) which has its own transport objects and can drift from the PFCG roles that reference it if not moved in lockstep, and BTP role collections/scopes defined in the extension apps' security descriptors and deployed via the BTP CI/CD pipeline entirely outside the ABAP transport system. The governance model needs a central layer defining naming conventions, an enterprise SoD ruleset that spans both ABAP and BTP-side authorizations, and a единый identity/IGA process that provisions consistently across all of it, while each S/4 instance retains authority to author its own role content within that framework. Critically, the architecture must explicitly map how a single business role change propagates correctly across all three content types and transport paths at once - a common real-world failure mode is a PFCG role transport landing in production while its referenced Fiori catalog content or corresponding BTP scope assignment lags behind, leaving users in a broken intermediate state.
followUps: How would you detect and alert on drift between a PFCG role transport and its referenced Fiori content transport landing at different times?
How do you extend a single enterprise SoD ruleset to cover BTP-side authorizations that don't use classic authorization objects at all?
commonMistakes: Treating PFCG role transport, Fiori content transport, and BTP deployment as a single synchronized process without an explicit architecture ensuring they land together
Designing SoD rules only for classic ABAP objects and ignoring BTP-side scopes entirely
scenario: A multinational runs three regional S/4HANA instances plus several BTP-based extension apps, and users intermittently land in a broken state where their role is active but the corresponding Fiori tiles or BTP app access lag behind by days.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Central Governance Framework] --> B[Naming Convention + SoD Ruleset]
  A --> C[Identity/IGA Provisioning Process]
  C --> D[S/4 Instance 1: PFCG Roles]
  C --> E[S/4 Instance 2: PFCG Roles]
  C --> F[BTP Extension Apps: Role Collections]
  D --> G[ABAP Transport: Roles + Fiori Content]
  F --> H[BTP CI/CD: Scopes + Role Collections]
  G -.drift risk.- H

question: Design row-level security for a custom Fiori app where users must be restricted simultaneously by company code (classic org value) and by a custom characteristic exposed only through embedded analytics.
hints: You'll likely need both a classic authorization object and an analysis authorization working together, not one replacing the other
Think about where each mechanism is actually evaluated
expectedAnswer: Layer a classic authorization object (company code) enforced via DCL for the transactional/OData consumption path with an analysis authorization for the custom characteristic enforced through RSECADMIN for the analytical consumption path, ensuring both are provisioned together from the same identity governance trigger so a user's restriction is consistent regardless of which part of the app they're using.
detailedAnswer: The classic org-value restriction (company code) is best implemented through DCL on the underlying CDS view, referencing a standard authorization object like F_BKPF_BUK via the pfcg_auth aspect, so every transactional/OData consumer of the view is filtered consistently. The custom characteristic, if it's only meaningful in the context of an aggregated analytical query (say, a custom profitability segment used purely for reporting), needs its own analysis authorization defined against that characteristic's InfoObject and maintained through RSECADMIN, since the OLAP engine evaluating the analytical part of the app doesn't consult DCL/classic authorization objects for that layer. The architectural discipline that makes this work long-term is provisioning both restrictions from a single trigger in the identity governance process - when a user's scope changes, the workflow updates both the classic org value assignment and the analysis authorization value assignment atomically, rather than treating them as two separate manual security tasks that can drift apart.
followUps: What would you test to prove both restrictions are actually enforced consistently across every part of the app?
How would you handle a characteristic that needs to be both a classic org-level field and an analytical dimension?
commonMistakes: Assuming a single authorization mechanism can cover both the transactional and analytical consumption paths of the same app
Provisioning the two restrictions through separate, unsynchronized processes
scenario: A custom profitability analysis Fiori app needs to restrict data by company code on its transactional detail view and by a custom profitability segment on its analytical summary view.

question: What is the difference between a 'restriction type' and an 'additional characteristic' in analysis authorizations, and what's the specific risk of granting 0BI_ALL?
hints: Restriction type controls how a value range is interpreted (single value, interval, pattern)
0BI_ALL removes restriction on every characteristic at once
expectedAnswer: Restriction type defines how values are compared for a given characteristic within an analysis authorization (single value, interval, pattern, or hierarchy node), while additional characteristics (like the always-injected 0TCAACTVT/0TCAIPROV) extend what's checked beyond the business characteristics you explicitly scoped; granting 0BI_ALL bypasses all characteristic restrictions entirely, functioning as the analytics equivalent of SAP_ALL.
detailedAnswer: When defining an analysis authorization in RSECADMIN, each characteristic can be restricted using a specific restriction type - single values, intervals, patterns with wildcards, or hierarchy node authorization for characteristics with a hierarchy - and getting this wrong (e.g., using pattern matching where a hierarchy-node restriction was intended) can silently grant broader access than designed. Every analysis authorization automatically includes technical characteristics like 0TCAACTVT (activity) and 0TCAIPROV (InfoProvider), which are additional characteristics injected by the system rather than ones you explicitly modeled, and forgetting they exist is a common source of confusion when a query behaves differently than expected. 0BI_ALL is a special reserved authorization that grants full, unrestricted access to every characteristic and every InfoProvider - functionally equivalent to SAP_ALL for the analytics stack - and it is routinely over-granted during go-live crunches to unblock testing, then never scoped down, becoming a standing audit finding.
followUps: How would you detect which users currently hold 0BI_ALL and assess whether it's still justified?
What's a realistic remediation approach for scoping down 0BI_ALL without breaking legitimate reporting access?
commonMistakes: Not accounting for automatically-injected technical characteristics when troubleshooting unexpected query authorization results
Leaving 0BI_ALL assigned post-go-live as a permanent shortcut
scenario: An audit finds that 40 users still hold the 0BI_ALL analysis authorization eighteen months after go-live.
